title: be going to (do)
desc: 계획된 의도 또는 근거 있는 예측
examples: >> I've done a lot of hiking in Korea. I did Jirisan and my first hike ever in Korea was Sarakan. We started at 3:00 a.m. I thought we were going to be the only crazy people on the mountain and there was a line going up the mountain.
Watch your step. It's a little steep here. Going down is really hard on my knees. I feel like they're going to give out. It's a little slippery, too. Wa!
I know this is not going to look cool on camera. Okay, trail running.

John >> food at the bottom of a mountain is always the best. I love acorn jelly and I heard that it's handmade here, so it must be great. I also got some dongju. I love traditional Korean alcohol, but I'm not going to lie, it always gives me a killer headache the next day. I hope that's not the case tomorrow. Fingers crossed. Cheers.
I'm going to enjoy the rest of my meal.

title: will / shall
examples: Fit check. I have a zip up on because I always like to dress in layers while hiking. I have a hat on and a backpack full of supplies. And of course, I wore my hiking boots. I'm ready. Let's check the trail map to see which route I'll be taking. I want to go to the peak today.

title: can / could / be able to
examples: I think we went the wrong way. You can eat the butt.
A hiking necessity. Gimbop, you can eat the butt. I don't like the butt. It's too big.
Whoa. I can see the apartments. Oh. Oh, there's a really pretty bird. I think it's good for bird watching.
>> Oh, someone feeds you. Well, huh. Can we take him home?

title: must / can't (deduction)
examples: John >> food at the bottom of a mountain is always the best. I love acorn jelly and I heard that it's handmade here, so it must be great. I also got some dongju. I love traditional Korean alcohol, but I'm not going to lie, it always gives me a killer headache the next day. I hope that's not the case tomorrow. Fingers crossed. Cheers.

title: have to / must
examples: It's kind of an unspoken rule. If somebody asks that, you have to say you're almost there. Even if you're very far. Am I almost to the top? You're almost there. You're almost there.
We made it to the peak. Woohoo. I have to take a picture with the marker for proof.
Before I went on the Appalachian Trail, I had to get leave no trace certified.

title: should (advice)
examples: Goang Bong. That's where we're headed today. And we are here. Okay. Our course is about 2 kilometers. And it says it should take about an hour, but I guess that depends on how many snack breaks I take. Let's go.

title: would (habit/conditional)
examples: This is such an amazing view. And the leaves are changing colors. It reminds me of home. Michigan has beautiful fall colors. I was hoping we'd catch them this year. Yes.

title: Passive (been done / being done)
examples: John >> food at the bottom of a mountain is always the best. I love acorn jelly and I heard that it's handmade here, so it must be great. I also got some dongju. I love traditional Korean alcohol, but I'm not going to lie, it always gives me a killer headache the next day. I hope that's not the case tomorrow. Fingers crossed. Cheers.
